title: Chapter 5: The Algorithm
content: Day three of my embedded journalism — my term, not hers — and Cliff House has already started to feel like a habitat I could mutate to fit.

The guest office is a glassCube cantilevered over the cliff edge, desk positioned so the Pacific fills your entire peripheral vision like a screensaver that never repeats. Jax gave it to me with a shrug and a comment about good light for video editing. She meant it as convenience. I've repurposed it as a surveillance post.

I'm supposed to be cutting footage. The B-roll from the party alone could fill a three-part docuseries — Jordan Keeler's manic lens work, the green laser splitting the night, the yacht moored at the edge of the frame like it was waiting for its cue. I have timeline drafts started in three different editing suites. None of them are open.
Instead, I'm running analytics.

It started as a background project, something to keep my hands busy while the footage rendered. I uploaded Jax's entire Instagram archive — 847 posts spanning four years — into a data visualizer. Not the kind influencers use to track engagement. This is network analysis software, the kind I learned in grad school mapping extremist recruitment pipelines. Same math. Different prey.

The results bloom across my screen like bioluminescence: a constellation of posts, dates, locations, captions, all orbiting one geographic center. I lean in. My breath fogs the glass of my laptop, and I sit back, blink, look again.
Not Silicon Valley. Not San Francisco. Not even downtown LA, where the venture capital flows like tap water.
The concentration point is a 40-meter radius. A marina slip at Marina del Rey.
Where the yacht is.

Every photo. Every story. Every carefully composed shot of crypto conferences and rooftop sunsets and Jax Vance standing at the intersection of power and beauty — all of it spiraling around an empty slip where a boat waits. The algorithm isn't optimizing for engagement. It's optimizing for proximity.

My fingers hover over the keyboard. The click of keys feels louder in this glass box suspended over nothing. I should feel triumphant. I should feel like a journalist who just found the thread that unravels the sweater. Instead, my stomach tightens, because the shape taking form on my screen isn't a marketing strategy. It's a love letter written in metadata.
I run the audience analytics next.

Jax's follower demographics are what you'd expect: young, female, crypto-curious, geographically scattered across the usual tech corridors — Austin, Miami, Lisbon. The primary cluster forms a dense, predictable mass. But my eye catches something at the periphery. A secondary cluster, smaller, quieter, almost invisible against the primary's noise.
Engaged users in the 25-35 female bracket. Located in Bel Air. Pacific Palisades. Brentwood.
Old money ZIP codes.
I filter for frequency. Most of this cluster engages intermittently — a like here, a view there, the casual scroll of someone who doesn't need to care. But one user breaks the pattern completely.
Private account. No profile picture. No bio.
This user has liked every single Jax Vance post within ninety seconds of posting. For two years.

Seven hundred and twelve posts. Seven hundred and twelve likes, each arriving within the window it would take someone who had push notifications enabled and a finger already hovering over the screen. Not a fan. Not a bot. A watcher. Someone who doesn't just follow — someone who waits.
My breath catches. The Pacific churns below me, indifferent, and I watch the data pulse on my screen like a heartbeat that doesn't know it's being monitored.
I find Jax in the server room.

It's a climate-controlled vault in the basement of Cliff House, and I mean vault literally — steel door, biometric lock, air so cold my lungs seize for a half-second when I walk in. Screens line the walls, each one cycling through blockchain transactions in real-time: hash values cascading, wallet balances fluctuating, the entire nervous system of a financial empire humming at a frequency I can almost feel in my teeth.

Jax is standing in the center of it, coffee cup steaming in one hand, the other hand braced against a server rack. She's watching the screens the way other people watch rain — not tracking any single droplet, just absorbing the pattern. The hot coffee rises in tendrils of steam that the cold air snatches apart.
She hears me and doesn't turn. "You're supposed to be editing."
"I was."
"Past tense is doing a lot of work in that sentence."

I don't ask permission. That's the thing about Day three — the formalities have burned off, and what's left is just two women in a room with the truth between them like a table neither of us is sitting at yet.
I turn my laptop around.

The data visualizer is still running. The constellation of posts. The geographic center pulsing at Marina del Rey. And layered over it, the engagement map — one invisible thread connecting seven hundred and twelve likes to a single private account that never misses.
"You're not posting for the world," I say. "You're posting for one person."

The silence stretches. It fills the server room the way the cold does — seeping into corners, pressing against skin. The blockchain screens keep humming, indifferent to human crisis. A transaction worth 2.3 million dollars clears somewhere in the cascade, and nobody cares.
Jax sets down her coffee. The ceramic clicks against the metal server rack, too loud.
She doesn't deny it.
"The algorithm isn't marketing," I say. "It's a homing beacon."

Jax's jaw tightens. Not anger — something rawer. The expression of someone whose architecture has been read, whose load-bearing walls have been identified. She looks at my screen like it's an X-ray of her chest cavity.
"You're good," she says. Not a compliment. An acknowledgment of exposure.
"How long has it been?"
"Long enough."
"That's not an answer."
"No," she says. "It isn't."
She picks up her coffee again. Takes a sip. Sets it down. The steam has stopped rising.
"Come with me."

Jax's private study is on the top floor of Cliff House, behind a door I haven't seen opened before. The room is smaller than I expected — a desk, a chair, a single window facing the bay. No screens. No servers. Just paper, pens, and a sleek black device the size of a thumb drive resting on a granite coaster.
A cold wallet.

I know what it is because I've researched enough crypto infrastructure to recognize the form factor. Offline storage. The private key exists only on this device, never touching a network, never vulnerable to a hack. Whatever's on this wallet is the kind of secret you keep in a vault that has no door to the internet.
Jax sits. She doesn't offer me a seat. I stand.

She picks up the cold wallet. It weighs almost nothing — a few grams of plastic and silicon — but the way she holds it, you'd think it was made of something that could shatter. She plugs it into her laptop.
The screen prompts for a passphrase. Jax types, and a 32-character hexadecimal key appears.

I read the characters aloud without thinking. It's what I do — I read things out loud because the sound of my own voice has always been the way I make information real. The first six characters leave my mouth before my brain catches up to what they are.

The rest blurs into alphanumeric noise, but those first six are enough. The green code from the party. The signal. It was never random. It was a name and a date, compressed into hex, broadcast on a laser to anyone looking — and meant for exactly one person who would know.
Jax is quiet for a long time. The bay outside the window is dark. No green laser tonight. The signal is off, and the silence it leaves is heavier than light.
"It's her birthday," Jax says. "July 12th. Vivienne Sterling."
She says the name like it costs her something. Like each syllable is a coin she's dropping into a meter that's been running for six years and shows no sign of stopping.
"I built all of this for her."

The admission is not proud. It's exhausted. I watch her face and I see the architecture of a woman who has spent six years constructing an empire for the same reason medieval builders raised cathedrals — not because God would necessarily come, but because the act of building was the only prayer that felt like enough.

Every post. Every deal. Every climb up the capital ladder from nothing to everything. The blockchain, the brand, the cliff house, the yacht — all of it, every brick and beam and byte, laid at the feet of a woman who married someone else.
The cold wallet sits in Jax's palm. I watch her thumb trace its edge. She's not looking at me. She's looking at the dark water where the signal usually lives.
"What if she doesn't come back?"

The question leaves me before I can weigh it. I've been avoiding it since the beach, since I put down my camera and made the choice to see Jax as a person instead of a subject. Questions like this — questions that reach into the soft tissue of someone's life — are the ones a documentarian is supposed to save for the final act, when the footage is already locked and the subject can't take it back.
But I'm not just a documentarian anymore. I haven't been since I chose to stay.

Jax doesn't flinch. She doesn't deflect. She looks at the bay, and the bay looks back, and the silence between them is the silence of two things that have been waiting for each other so long they've forgotten who started.
"Then I'll keep building until she does." Her voice is steady. Not brave — just factual. "Or until there's nothing left to build with."

I want to call it obsession. I want to apply the clinical language I learned in school — codependency, fixation, the architecture of attachment disorder. That's what my training says. That's what the framework demands.

But I'm standing in a room with a woman who turned love into infrastructure. Who encoded a birthday into a blockchain and fired it across a bay every night like a lighthouse for a ship that might never sail home. And the word that fits isn't obsession.
It's devotion.

And there's a difference. Obsession consumes. Devotion constructs. Obsession is the fire that burns the house down. Devotion is the one that keeps rebuilding it, room by room, even when no one's coming home. I don't know if the difference matters to anyone but Jax. But standing here, watching her hold a cold wallet like it holds the only pulse she trusts, I know it matters to me.

I walk back to the cottage along the cliff path. The night is clear and cold. My laptop bag presses against my shoulder blade with each step, and I swear it's heavier than it was this morning. Knowledge does that. It accrues weight the longer you carry it.

I'm not just documenting anymore. I know Jax's secret now — not the performance of it, not the brand narrative, but the raw, private, devastating engine underneath. And that knowledge is a weapon I didn't ask for and don't know how to put down.
The cottage is dark when I reach it. I don't turn on the lights. I sit on the edge of the bed and open my phone, fingers moving on autopilot to the data I downloaded earlier.
The private account. The one that liked every post within ninety seconds. The one with no face and no bio and two years of unwavering attention.
I open the profile.

The screen glows in the dark room. The bio is empty. The follower count is 3 — three accounts this private user allows to see her. And the account name sits at the top of the screen like a signature on a letter that was never meant to be mailed.
V.S. Vivienne Sterling. And 1492 — a year, a voyage, someone setting out across an ocean not knowing what they'd find.
I screenshot the profile. The image saves to my camera roll with a soft, final click.
The signal goes both ways.

Vivienne isn't just the woman on the yacht, the distant object of Jax's devotion, the green light at the end of the bay. She's been watching. For two years, she's been standing at her own window, pressing her own finger to the glass, liking every post like a heartbeat she can't stop and can't explain. She's reading the homing beacon. And maybe — maybe — she's been waiting for it to reach her.
I set the phone on the nightstand. The screen dims, then darkens. Outside, the Pacific folds over itself, wave after wave, building and breaking and building again.
The algorithm worked.
The only question left is what happens when the beacon finds its mark.
